summary.brief: Lyon v. Riverside Methodist Hosp.. Medical malpractice case involving paediatrics.

evidence_index.text: On or about May 27, 2015, Lyon presented at Lower Lights Christian Health Center with a history of GERD and tachycardia.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: In October 2015, Lyon presented again at Lower Lights with complaints of gastroparesis. Lyon had lost over 30 pounds since her last visit.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On November 24, 2015, Lyon presented to the Riverside Methodist Hospital, reporting that she had not been able to keep water down for several days as well as intractable vomiting with nausea for several weeks.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Lyon was readmitted to Riverside two days later with persistent nausea and positional vertigo with spinning, as well as diplopia and blurred vision.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On January 15, 2016, Lyon was admitted to the Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center where she was diagnosed with severe nutritional deficiency and Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome, which led to severe peripheral neuropathy.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court found the noneconomic damages cap in R.C. 2323.43 unconstitutional as it violated both Lyon’s right to due process and equal protection.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The jury rendered its verdict in favor of Lyon and awarded damages of $25,172,526.32.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The jury also determined that Makkar, Reddy, and Gaible were each 3 percent responsible for Lyon’s injuries and damages while Ohio Gastroenterology Group, Inc. was jointly liable for 9 percent.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We find the instant case more analogous to Kalitan than Oliver. Unlike Oliver, this matter concerns a private litigant and not a political subdivision.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: Accordingly, as applied to the facts of this case, R.C. 2323.43 is unconstitutional under the equal protection provision of the Ohio Constitution.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.chief_complaint: Intractable vomiting and nausea for several weeks
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient reports not being able to keep water down for several days and has been in and out of the emergency department for two months receiving IV fluids.

focused_exam.neurological: Positional vertigo with spinning, diplopia, and blurred vision

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Severe nausea and vomiting with possible nutritional deficiency

requestables.name: Thiamine Level
requestables.description: Measurement of thiamine (Vitamin B1) level in blood

reveal.decision_impact: Would guide immediate supplementation and prevent neurological complications

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to Riverside Methodist Hospital with severe symptoms

key_events.event: Patient reports intractable vomiting and nausea

timeline_phases.name: Diagnostic Workup
timeline_phases.description: Evaluation and testing to determine cause of symptoms

key_events.event: Patient discharged without thiamine level check

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with severe nausea, vomiting, and signs of nutritional deficiency
decision_points.prompt: What laboratory test should be ordered to investigate the cause of the patient's symptoms?

options.clinical_reasoning: Thiamine deficiency is a known cause of Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and should be ruled out in this clinical context.
Does not address the specific risk of thiamine deficiency given the patient's symptoms.

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant did not order thiamine level

expected_action_court.description: Thiamine level should have been ordered
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established the need to rule out thiamine deficiency

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to order thiamine level led to missed diagnosis of Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Thiamine deficiency should have been identified and treated early

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and severe peripheral neuropathy

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to diagnose thiamine deficiency
Causation established - earlier diagnosis would have prevented neurological injury

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Thiamine supplementation would have prevented Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome

factual_timeline.event: Patient presents to Riverside Methodist Hospital
Patient discharged from Riverside Methodist Hospital
